text: Okay. Hello, good evening to everybody. It's a pleasure to be here to... to perform a radical prostatectomy.

You know, I don't know if the case has been introduced. We are going to perform a robotic-assisted nerve-sparing prostatectomy, but the patient has three positive biopsies on the right side, at least four plus three. So we would not do a nerve-sparing on the right side, but we do a nerve-sparing on the left side.
I think it's a good opportunity to demonstrate the different techniques of wide excision and nerve-sparing in one patient.

Regarding the lymph nodes, here the philosophy is if the Briganti score is not higher than five or six, they don't do the lymph nodes. Briganti score is three in this patient, so they don't do lymph nodes.
In my hospital, we would do the lymph nodes, but this is debatable, and I think we should follow the rules of this hospital.
Okay, I have to thank the anesthetist who is sitting next to us to do the anesthesia. My colleague is Dr. Dietl, she is assisting.

We are using an X system, which is very comparable to the SI system. Yeah, there's nothing special about this. Here, they do a docking between the legs. At home, we do a side docking. But it doesn't matter at all where you dock the DaVinci, I think.
So I think the side docking is easier. But we should not change a winning team. And they used to do it here, so we should do that.

I think that you're absolutely right. We always shoot the way that we have learned. I would say the most safest way. For me, this is a very safe way to get access to the optimal cavity. And it works for all ports. So we do like that. Yeah, you're right.
If you want, you can also go directly. But for beginners, I wouldn't recommend. If you start with... laparoscopy of robotic, I would say the mini laparotomy is a very safe access.

This is the disadvantage of the trans access. You have to create the access to the reacher space, but as I said, we only do extra if...

The patient doesn't require lymph nodes and the space is a little bit limited compared to the transperitoneal access. And what we did find out is that we have three times more metastasis when we go trans in patients with intermediate risk. So we completely switched in patients where we do lymph nodes to the trans access. But it doesn't take long and it's also a nice exercise for beginners to bring the peritoneum down.
It's a nice, as I said, module one in the modular training to learn radical prostatectomy.

You have a very big experience in laparoscopic radical prostatectomy. What is the role of laparoscopic instead of robotic radical prostatectomy now in your vision? That's a very good question. To answer this, you know, we did in the last three years, we had running the only prospective randomized trial, multicenter, patient-blinded comparing lab prostatectomy versus robotic prostatectomy. And we included seven hundred eighty patients and we finished the recruitment three months ago. And we will come out with the data next year, but I can tell you the results are the first, the primary outcome was the continence, and secondary outcome, oncology, potency, and all this.

There was a clear difference. I can't give you the results now because it's not allowed to do, but I must say it was clearer than I expected. The continence is much better in one group. And we still do lab in my hospital, but this is more due to the fact that we have one robot permanently and we have a second only on two days. So we are fighting for a second all the week. So we have to do lab cases because we have to operate our patients. So this is the vase here, see that?

the patient's not so obese and it wouldn't be difficult to perform a lymphadenectomy here. So I don't know if this was a clear answer to your question, but I tried to answer. You didn't say what, I mean, which one was the winner? Yeah, I think it's clear.

But I remember that three or four years ago, we had the same discussion, and the impression was that good laparoscopic surgeon had the same result as with the robotic. Now, it seems to be some differences. But I don't know. I know that in the Martini Clinic, they are doing a very big randomized study on this with not so clear result in the outcome of the different patients. But they compare open versus robotic, don't they? Open laparoscopic and robotic. But I'm a genie, I think they don't do lap. They do mostly open laparoscopic.

As I said, I would do a wide excision on the right side or partly nerve sparing, but we can do an intrafascial nerve sparing on the left side. In my hospital, we also would do, the camera is not really in, I can't go further in. Can you improve this?

In my hospital, you could also, you know, if patients are very young and very extremely keen on potency, we could also offer nerve spurring on the right side. But in that situation, we should offer frozen sections. We started many, many years ago to do frozen sections. Actually, we do it in every patient. But especially in patients with Gleason four plus three and you do nerve spurring, I think it's a must to have frozen section if you do nerve spurring.

The surgeon confirms the bladder neck is clear and begins the prostatectomy. The bladder is empty, and the optic is adjusted for better visualization. The bladder neck is identified, with the bladder overlapping the prostate laterally.

The surgeon uses an instrument to palpate and differentiate the mobile bladder neck from the solid prostate. The ProGrasp is used to pull slightly, revealing the bladder neck and the longitudinal muscle fibers running into the prostatic urethra.

The surgeon performs blunt dissection around the urethra, extending laterally while maintaining a distance from the nerves. The bladder neck is preserved, and the dissection is completed circumferentially around the urethra.

The surgeon opens the bladder neck using cold scissors, avoiding the use of energy to prevent damage to the sphincter. A catheter is visible in the surgical field, and the incision is made at the bladder neck.
The surgeon navigates a conflict of instruments while identifying the correct surgical plane, avoiding entry into the adenomectomy plane during the prostatectomy procedure.

So this patient, he had a PET scan, actually. So showing no metastases and all this. And I think he also had an MRI.

And he did not show any... No, it's only a CT scan. A CT scan, yeah. So, yeah, that's... You know, most of our patients, and it is now getting a standard, they do multi-parametric MRIs and fusion biopsies, and then you have an MRI.

So most of the patients. But to tell you the truth, in one third of the patients, we can't agree during the surgery what they described in the MRI. So it's not so precise as I would love to have it. So you don't trust very much if he says there is no capsular disease? It's not the right saying to say I don't trust. I'm critical with the MRI, and I don't believe one hundred percent in the MRI.

Do you agree that by doing the nerve sparing, let's say from behind, finding the right cleavage plane where you are now, the nerve sparing is much, much easier? What is the rationale to stay medially to the ligament? We published paper for laparoscopy, for classic laparoscopy, where we did show that the preserving of the ligaments helps to improve early continence. So at the end, I will also fix, when we cut the bladder neck, there's also endopelvic fascia here. We will do a suturing from here to here.
